The gameplay was smooth, loading times were actually slightly faster than with Windows which was surprising to me. I even copied my game folder from my previous Windows 10 installation that had NVSE and all my mods which all worked! Was very surprised at all this.

title: Fallout: New Vegas

notes:
No problems or extra configuration related to Proton/Wine/whatever else at all. I had to go rootin' around through my FalloutPrefs.ini but that's because of the lack of OOTB Ultrawide support which is just as problematic under Windows.

However, once I had that figured out I played through the entirety of the Goodsprings part of the game with no issues running the vanilla game completely maxed out...now to try mods because I hear New California works with Proton! :)
title: Fallout: New Vegas

notes: Game runs perfectly. No stuttering, graphical weirdness (although HDR tends to cause some flickering on distant scenery, so switch it off and use bloom instead) or sound issues. In-game radio stations function correctly, NPC audio works as intended. Performance easily comparable to Windows.
title: Fallout: New Vegas

notes: DX9 to OpenGL translation may result in some graphical differences compared to playing on Windows, but this is common in DX9 only games on Proton so far.  Radeon users can improve the look and performance with Gallium Nine and a forked Proton with Gallium Nine Extras.  Otherwise, game patches and mods work as well as they do on Windows.
title: Fallout: New Vegas

title: Fallout: New Vegas

notes:
Older hardware, but NV still suffers from physics calculations that can slow VATS down to a crawl. I would use the more recent drivers but my older graphics card does not seem to support them.

It plays well enough until multiple opponents are on the scene: "See Ghost Town Gunfight". When multiple combatants are on the screen the game slows down considerably to unplayability until the combat is resovled. Otherwise very impressed and thankful for Proton. Saves me the effort of dual booting or PlayOnLinux and virtual drives.

title: Fallout: New Vegas

notes: Flawless. D9VK gives worse performance than stock DXVK, as a heads up in case you want to try it. Radio New Vegas is the only radio station that works as soon as you start up the game. The others take a little while to kick in (about 30 secs). Turn off the on-screen effects like Bloom and HDR to fix screen glitches and broken AA. Can't alt-tab out of the game if running in fullscreen - Works if running windowed. Tested with the 4GB patch, CASM, NVAC, MCM and Enhanced Camera. Game ran comparable to, if not better than, Windows. Solid Proton title.
title: Fallout: New Vegas

notes: Runs great, no tweaks.

notes: Everything works well except HDR and alt+tab in fullscreen mode.
title: Fallout: New Vegas

notes: Don't Alt+Tab if you play in fullscreen (if you play in Windowed then it doesn't matter) and disable HDR as it causes graphical flickering with dust. Both of these issues can be present on Windows though mostly on old hardware, so it's just a result of the game's engine being so old and not a fault of Proton necessarily. I installed a bunch of mods such as NVAC, 4GB patcher, NMC's texture pack (the 2048x2048 version), CASM, MCM, and the PipBoy 3000 reskin. These all worked. Radio stations work though they can be a little fiddly at times. Again, though, this can happen on Windows. It just seems to happen more consistently with Proton just like with the other issues.
title: Fallout: New Vegas

title: Fallout: New Vegas

notes: Everything works fine, except fights. In them, the FPS fell to unplayable 1-5. It seems like me solved this problem by writing the line bDisableAllGore = 1 in the file Fallout_default.ini, in [General] (turns off blood and dismemberment). After that, everything worked stable at 60 fps.
title: Fallout: New Vegas

cpu: AMD A8-7100 Radeon R5, 8 Compute Cores 4C+4G
notes:
More or less same as Fallout 3

I modified the Fallout_default.ini file in the game folder with the following parameters: For the random crashes and freezes [General] bUseThreadedAI=1 iNumHWThreads=2 For the mouse speed [Controls] fForegroundMouseAccelBase=0 fForegroundMouseAccelTop=0 fForegroundMouseBase=0 fForegroundMouseMult=0 For the lag in combat and using V.A.T.S. [Audio] bMultiThreadAudio=1 iAudioCacheSize=8192 The parameters for the audio have solved the problem of the drastic slowdown during the fights, making them finally fluid, but the sound tends to distort and delay, furthermore at the exit the game crashes and it must be forced to close, so i had to play in a window to avoid the restart of Cinnamon.
title: Fallout: New Vegas

notes: As previous reports mentioned, adding various entries to the "Fallout_default.ini" file can fix issues. The only one I experienced was the mouse issue, which can be fixed via "fForegroundMouseAccelBase=0 fForegroundMouseAccelTop=0 fForegroundMouseBase=0 fForegroundMouseMult=0" as another report mentioned. Tabbing out in fullscreen doesn't work, and running in windowed mode breaks the Steam overlay (no Shift+Tab and no Steam Controller, big rip). It also causes the game to hang on exit and you have to manually kill the FalloutNV.exe process (I use KSysGuard but any System Monitor or equivalent Terminal command will work too). Performance is pretty much the same as Windows, just turn off HDR and Bloom (not even D9VK fixes this, and D9VK gives worse performance anyway in my tests). Trying to use galliumnine just borks the game for me so I couldn't test performance. The best part though: I was able to use the Vortex Mod Manager (previously known as Nexus Mod Manager) to install my Fallout mods. Enabling the script files sometimes doesn't work and I have to do that from the Fallout: New Vegas launcher, but I think setting load order works. I installed 0.17.X through Lutris (see here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MDr7L-XRd54) but ***install Vortex on the same drive as your game or else you'll get a nasty error message about failed deployment.*** This is a result of EXDEV not being able to link files across drives/partitions. After you install Vortex on the correct drive, install the mods as you normally would for New Vegas, but without the glorious Mod Organizer 2. We can hope for WINE support someday. Profiles for New Vegas also work with Vortex, and you can switch between them with ease. NVSE and JIP NP NVSE both work, which are dependencies for many mods. The 4GB patcher works (I ran it using my system WINE installation of 4.11). On top of this, I tested NVAC, MCM, CASM, Enhanced Camera, Clarity, PipBoy 3000 Reskin, NMC's Texture Pack (2048x2048) and the Enhanced Loading Screens. Texture packs had no impact on performance, nor did any of the mods. The game seems to run much more stable through WINE/Proton, which is interesting. Either way, all the standard New Vegas mods will work when either installed manually or installed through Vortex. Excellent experience! Pity about the tabbing out, but alt+tab is kinda broken on Windows, too.
title: Fallout: New Vegas

notes: Played from start to finish. Lowering anisotropic filtering was required in order to fix the V.A.T.S black screen (an in-game feature). sometimes the HUD glitches out depending on where you are looking at, mainly water related elements such as lakes, also haircut and "sepia filter" will go away when the HUD is glitched, but usually goes back to normal in few seconds and rarely happens. Playing in windowed mode was needed in order to successfully switch Tabs, otherwise if playing in Fullscreen mode when switching back to the game tab it'll refuse to regain focus making it impossible to interact with the game until you manually kill the process and you launch the game again. Few crashes happened, specifically at the ""repconn test site"". Frametimes and framerates were a-okay smooth, with a stable 60FPS experience but explosions (only those generated by items and NOT in-game events) will make the game run at 5 or so FPS for a few seconds and as far as i can tell, only the first times it happens, for example after launching the game once the process was previously killed.

notes: Game performance is solid, no need for D9VK (actually worsens performance). Audio will sometimes take a little bit to pop in from the PipBoy but this happens on Windows too. You can't tab out if you play the game in fullscreen as the game won't gain focus again. Bit of a pain, but oh well. Tested with a ton of mods (NMC's Texture Pack, CASM, MCM, NVAC, PipBoy 3000 Reskin, to name a few) and they didn't impact game stability from my tests. Use Vortex Mod Manager to apply these mods, or apply on Windows and boot the game using WINE. Disable HDR/Bloom to get rid of graphical effects. I didn't have any issue with VATs and never have, for what it's worth. Your mileage my vary though

title: Fallout: New Vegas

notes:
Runs very well, It can be played without tweaks, but the mouse will feel a bit weird and might be some very minor graphical glitches. I have tested some mods, But I installed them manually and it works just fine. 

To Fix the Mouse and Graphical Glitches:
Disable HDR and Bloom in the Launcher.
Add these into your Fallout_Default.ini under [Controls]:
fForegroundMouseAccelBase=0
fForegroundMouseAccelTop=0
fForegroundMouseBase=0
fForegroundMouseMult=0

I am also using a multi-monitor setup and have had no issues with it
title: Fallout: New Vegas

title: Fallout: New Vegas

notes:
A simple guide to getting Fallout New Vegas to run on Linux using Steam Proton

Step 1 – Install Fallout New Vegas using Proton version 4.11-3

Step 2 – Edit the Fallout_default.ini to fix the mouse acceleration problem. 

This can be found in Home / .steam / steam / steamapps / common / Fallout New Vegas

You need to add the following under the [Controls] section.

fForegroundMouseAccelBase=0
fForegroundMouseAccelTop=0 
fForegroundMouseBase=0 
fForegroundMouseMult=0

Save the ini file after inserting this. 

Step 3 – Options Tweaks

Disable both HDR and Anti-Alialiasing to prevent graphic issues. 

Once done launch the game and enjoy. 

See https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jzkZaj5oWsQ for a visual tutorial.

title: Fallout: New Vegas

notes: Mostly smooth performance and doesn't crash at all. I say mostly, because when the cinematic view of each kill happens, the fps drops temporarily until the games goes back to normal fps when the cinematic view ends. This tended to be the worst when the slow motion kill happened in first person. Also, sometimes, depending on the direction the camera is pointing in first person, it seems like the shaders break and the game ui, hand and weapon models, and the yellow hue disappears and a blue tint can be seen (may be the base color before the shaders are applied). This disappearance is usually corrected by moving the camera to another direction, typically opposite or 45 degrees left or right of the aforementioned direction. Also, if the game is set to full screen and alt + tab is performed, it seems impossible to get the window back into focus, no matter how many times the window is put into the foreground. This requires the game to be forcefully closed and launched again to be able to interact with it again. Overall, better experience than on Windows unmodded due to lack of crashes.
title: Fallout: New Vegas

notes: The game ran fine out-of-the-box, but having antialiasing enabled with HDR makes graphical artifacts such as white boxes or flashes appear during gameplay.  This mostly seems to go away (but not 100%) by using d9vk (by changing the game's launch command to this: PROTON_USE_D9VK=1 %command%)...  The radio and everything else seem to work fine for me, but I haven't played over an hour yet.

notes: Works perfectly out-of-the-box. For mod-heavy runs, or when playing on a lower-end machine, performance can be improved by using D9VK. On Proton 4.11, this is achieved by adding this (no quotes) to the game's launch options: PROTON_USE_D9VK=1 %command%
